moment_text: So these were sort of the negative kind of turning points that really hard, really dark and shadowy. But uh, I think when I was, you know. 19 or 20. I started to compete in the adult ranks for bodybuilding. And what I understood somehow at kind of a young age was that the mental fitness, the mental strength and wellness is just as if not more important than the physical body strength and wellness. And so I really started to dive into what it meant to have a strong, healthy mindset. And later the spiritual connection kind of came along with that. But it really, at first it was so I could be really great at my sport and I started to hang out with people like Oprah, like Tony Robbins, like, uh, Jack Kornfield, uh, Jim Rohn, you know, these sort of real leaders in, in the way of manifesting and vibration and connecting to the universe to get what it, whatever it is that you want. And so those were like my first teachers and sort of the first. Jumping off point of me starting to discover what it meant to be mentally well and fit so that I could be really good at my sport. And to be honest with you, I really think that this was my secret weapon that got me to be number two in the world because I'm five feet tall on stage 115 pounds. So like competing in a pro women's body bodybuilding division, like the stats don't really add up. But I had the energy, I had the belief, and I had the perseverance and the willpower. So I think that I had the universe behind me because of all the work that I did on my mind and my spirit to help me get to that point.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: The moment contains clear agency signals (seeking_information) and a system interaction (media).

moment_text: Getting arrested. That was a, a really dark time for me, um, with drinking. I had some really unresolved trauma under the surface, and it was, you know, the, the core of it was I was really betraying my soul. I was doing things. Because I wanted love. I wanted acceptance. I wanted validation. But at the core of it, my soul was like, no, you actually don't really wanna do these things. So of course you add alcohol into the mix and cue the big blow up and a couple nights in jail, and then another inflection point going, okay, what's really what? What am I really burying here? What's going on under the surface?
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: The moment contains clear emotional signals (shame, confusion) and agency signals (asking_questions).

moment_text: Quitting bodybuilding was after that couple years, and that was really, that was a dark time also because even though it was the right thing for me to do at the right time, I didn't realize how much my identity was like so wrapped up in that. And I think going through an identity crisis or existential, uh, shift, or whatever you wanna call it, is really challenging because we lose. Who we were, who we thought we were. I think that's the big thing, is like I thought I was this number two in the world, bodybuilder and I am, but that wasn't all of who I am, and it took me losing that identity and stripping everything away and becoming so lost and depressed and anxious and all the things that go with that. Really confused in order for me to build myself up again into who I. Who I am today, which is like now I have this understanding that I'm so much more than any identity or role I could ever place upon myself.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: The moment contains strong emotional signals (sadness, anxiety, confusion) and an agency signal (making_decisions).

moment_text: And then the last inflection point, and we can go into any of these if you'd like, but where I find myself today really working on my mental and spiritual wellness is for devotion. And, and in service. And so where I find myself now, we talked about it in the introduction. I hold space, I create experiences for people. I do retreats, I do ceremonies. I serve yoga, I serve sound. And so all of these things are a way for me to hold space and to be helpful. Now, my mental fitness and wellness is, is more like. How helpful do I wanna be? Because the more clear I can be, the cleaner space I can hold, the more I can be helpful to those around me. And that might be in a yoga class, in a sound ceremony, in a plant medicine ceremony. It could even be at the gas station or the grocery store, you know? So for me it's like. It started off really positive. Then you go through the dark night of the Soul, the hero's journey, and all of these different trials and tribulations. And now to end here, and I mean, this is just where I am now, I'm sure. I know that there's gonna be many more inflection points and things that are gonna push me deeper and deeper into myself and into mental wellness.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: The moment contains emotional signals (clarity, hope) and a system interaction (community).

moment_text: Many inflection points. I was gonna say lots of inflection points, and I just wanna say thank you for sharing both the ups and the downs. And I think what's really interesting, if you look at your journey back when you were 19 and 20, figuring out that the, the mindset was gonna be what got you to, to the top was really, that was the shift that was gonna be your competitive advantage. It was all focused on self and getting to that. And it's really interesting as I look at just the, the multiple. Inflection points you just suggested is where you are today is from self to others and really supporting others and I think being of service. So I think that's very interesting. I wanted to call attention to that, but I also wanna unpack.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: The moment contains emotional signals (validation, recognition) and a system interaction (peer_community).

moment_text: Good question. I love, and I love this story because you know, now when we look back at our stories, we can see how all the pieces were just so perfectly aligned, even though in the moment it didn't seem like they were my high school. Every year put on an annual bodybuilding show for like all the students and athletes. And I think back, it's so crazy. Like what high school even does that? But it was so perfect that I was going to that high school and that is where I got introduced. And so during those four years of high school, the first two years I won showmanship, which is like best, best performance, basically best energy, you know? And I was a cheerleader, so I could tumble, I could flip, I could do the jumps like, so I did all of that mixed in with the posing. And then the next. Two years, my junior, senior year I won overall Miss Wesley Chapel bodybuilder, like, so that was kind of the, the beginning of it. And I was, you know, 16. And you know, at the time I said I was cheerleading. So really cheerleading was my everything. I was on a couple different teams, my school team, an all star team, an adult team, and the captain of my school team. And so I was really, really cheerleading and in my mind what I, what I wanted was to go to college for cheerleading. I tried out for the cheerleading team, made the team, the coach said, if you get into the college you're in.

moment_text: Well, I did not get into the college. I missed my a CT test, the standardized testing by one point. And I think the universe had other plans for me, but at the time I was so upset and ended up going to the community college nearby. I still moved with my friends. We were all living in the same house. I just wasn't going to the same college as them.
eligibility_rationale: Passes evidence gate A2: has emotional signals and system_interaction is not 'none'.

moment_text: So talk about like another humbling moment, right? It's like, oh god, but. When I was at the community college, I ended up taking a personal training class, anatomy and physiology and nutrition and all of these other class. 'cause I was like, you know what? I do like this stuff too. I don't really know what I wanna go to college for. But the electives I chose were very that in the fitness world. And I started to think, oh, I really like. Anatomy. I love physiology. I love nutrition and what it does to the body. It was a whole science and was very clear to me, and I started to work out and. When I was 19, I competed in my very first show.

moment_text: And you know, this little story about being humbled, it's so funny. Like my journey, it's just like humble, humble moment after humble moment. I competed, it was in the teen division. There was only two of us on stage, me and this other girl, and they awarded me first place. Well then we go backstage and they say, Danny, I'm so sorry but you actually got second. So can you please come back on stage and actually give her the trophy and we'll give you the second place. So I had to march my little butt back on stage, hand her the first place trophy, except my second place trophy. And I went backstage and I cried. 'cause it's so, it's that high of like, oh my God, I won.
eligibility_rationale: Passes evidence gate A2: has emotional signals and system_interaction is not 'none'.

moment_text: Something about it. I think it was the devotion. It took the weeks of training my body and eating really good and training my mind and really. Devoting myself to this for a number of weeks because it's not like you can just go hard for a week and then fall off again and then do it. It has to be like all in devotion. So something about that really caught my attention and then I just kept going. Next, next show I did, I looked even better and I actually won first place and then the next show and yeah, and then it was just kind of, you know, that was the path from there.
eligibility_rationale: Passes evidence gate A2: has agency signals.

moment_text: Um, I guess I'll start with the moment that I decided to actually quit bodybuilding. Um, I was 27 and I had done my first mushroom journey, and so it. This was like the jumping off point to my work with plant medicine and seeing how wow, like how beneficial this is for me. Maybe not for everybody, but for me it was a big moment and I was competing. I had a birthday party and my friends came over with some mushrooms and we all took them and kind of did our own thing. It was really interesting and at one point I found myself in the bathroom looking at my own eyes in the mirror looking like deep into my pupil, and it was like. Like a fun house, almost like it was, uh, layers and layers of mirrors going back and back and back. Almost like I was looking into the timeline of my soul or something. Really. I'll never forget the feeling of that.
eligibility_rationale: Passes evidence gate A2: has agency signals.
notes_for_reviewer: The speaker mentions using mushrooms, which could be a sensitive topic, but it's framed as a therapeutic/spiritual experience rather than recreational drug use. No specific risk flag seems to fit perfectly.

moment_text: And I remember looking at myself and going, what do you want? What do you wanna do? Not what your coaches want you to do, not what all your fans want you to do, not what Instagram, not what your sponsors want. What do you wanna do? And at the time I wanted to stop eating meat. I was going through this big shift and you know, with bodybuilding it's very carnivorous, you know, you're eating meat seven times a day. And I started to kind of think, oh, I, I don't actually wanna hurt animals anymore and I'm eating so much of them. And it was just so that little piece started to break away and I was like, I actually don't want to eat meat. And then I had this vision during this mushroom ceremony. Me in front of a big mountain and my next thought was, I have to get up in the morning and do cardio. Like how am I gonna get to this Mount, Mount Fiji, or whatever I saw in my vision, if I have to get up in the morning and do cardio again and again and for years, and I just thought in a way I'm. Not that I'm wasting my youth in a gym, but it felt in that way. Like, okay, I, I think this has run its course for me. And for the first time ever in 10 years, I thought about doing something else other than fasted cardio, other than being on stage. And so that was the moment where I kind of had the vision and the empowerment and the idea to, to start to cut this outta my life.
eligibility_rationale: Passes evidence gate A2: has agency signals.

moment_text: So first I cut out the sponsors because honestly, I hated being sponsored. I hated it. I didn't like people telling me what to do when, Hey, I'm going to give you $2,000 a month to post about this protein powder. Even though you don't take it, I want you to come and stand. So I just, the money was helpful. It was really nice. People wanted to meet me to be on their team, but I did not like being sort of controlled in that way. So that was my first thing. I said quit the sponsors probably like two days after that mushroom ceremony. And then. A couple months later, bodybuilding kind of started to look very different to me and I ordered my suit and I went vegan by this point. And so I changed my suit color from red to green and I was always in red. That was kind of my like signature color that I loved, but I changed it to green 'cause I was now vegan and I said, I'm gonna compete for the vegans and show everybody that this can be done. I had a green suit and I named her Gaia, and I remembered I, I tried to compete, but it just wasn't, the fire wasn't there anymore.
eligibility_rationale: Passes evidence gate A2: has agency signals.

moment_text: It was this sort of killer instinct that was just like, at all costs, do whatever you need to do. And I remember the moment. Sending my suit back. 'cause I told my suit sponsor, I, I need a break. I, I don't know what's going on, but I can't get ready for the show and I'd like to send the suit back in case somebody else wants to use it. And I'll just let you know when I'm ready. And I remember the moment sending the suit back in the post office and it was kind of like time stood still as I was putting the suit into the priority shipping box. I like knew that was going to be the last suit I would ever touch, but I couldn't admit it. And so that started the. The sort of identity crisis for almost a year. I couldn't say I'm not competing anymore, even though I, I knew it somewhere inside, I couldn't admit it. And my nickname on Instagram was Little monster. Little Monster because I was little, but I was like super jacked and I liked the universe, so the stars, you know, and I tried to change my name to other things 'cause I didn't know what to go by anymore. If I'm not a little monster on stage, well then who am I? People who knew me by little Monster didn't even know me by Danny. They didn't even know my name. They didn't know I had a master's in exercise physiology. They didn't know that I was like a kind soul. All they, all they knew was that I looked a certain way, got second in the world and did whatever, whatever.
eligibility_rationale: Passes evidence gate A2: has barrier signals.
notes_for_reviewer: The speaker describes a profound identity crisis, which falls under mental health sensitivity.

moment_text: During this time where we quit body, where I quit bodybuilding, I went to selling health insurance. And I was telemarketing actually with these headphones. This was the only good thing that came outta selling health insurance other than a big slice of humble pie, because I went from living my dream number two in the world on the biggest stage that bodybuilding has to offer. And within a couple months, I was sitting in front of my computer, cold calling. People's houses to see if they wanted health insurance. And I was getting yelled at on the phone and I was really living a nightmare. Um, but I, you know, there was a part of me that knew I needed to go through that. And at the time my husband also quit his job because we were promised a lot of money selling health insurance and we had a big wedding to pay off and some competition expenses that were still in debt. And, um. We both were doing this health insurance thing and we were broke, we were fighting, we were not doing well, and we were not ourselves. We were not okay.
eligibility_rationale: Passes evidence gate A2: has barrier signals.

moment_text: And so it was time to do something else. I was like, we, we were on the brink of a divorce and so I said, okay, this is our Hail Mary. Let's move to Colorado. My sister was there. 'cause in my mind I needed to get away from Orlando, Florida. I needed to get away from my friends, away from the gym, away from my surroundings. That kept me a little monster and I needed to go somewhere else. And what felt really good to me was nature. I wanna be with the mountains. I wanna be with big trees. I wanna be with my sister just in case things with me and my husband don't work out. At least I have those things and I can build myself up from there.
eligibility_rationale: Passes evidence gate A2: has barrier signals.

moment_text: She said, Dan, Danny, if you come to Colorado, I can get you a job. And because I had the master's in exercise physiology, I could pretty much do anything personal training wise. I started working for her as a fitness, as a group fitness instructor making 30 bucks an hour or whatever I was saying, working for my little sister after I was number two in the world. It was just such an interesting turn of events and, and a bit humbling. But also quite freeing because nobody in that gym knew that I was little monster. I had dreadlocks. I didn't have as much muscle anymore, so I looked like a totally different person. So I could just really be me without any of that baggage or any of those accolades, any of that, anything.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 gate: has emotional signals and a system interaction (family).

moment_text: And then something happens and we look at identity crises and stuff. But interestingly, in a number of my conversations, one thing that has really come through, and I think you'll appreciate this, is that you do what you do, but that's not who you are. So it's like, I'm not my job. Um, you know, so your job was to be number two in the world. You were a bodybuilder to be the best you could be in the world at that, that was your job, but that wasn't who Danny was. And so I think that what's interesting about this phase of your life is really the ability to explore who Danny is. And, and now to your point of really going into deep devotion. I think sometimes we don't recognize, I myself had an inflection point that had me falling off there, jumping off the mountain for multiple reasons and, you know, rediscovering, or actually maybe not even rediscovering, discovering for the first time who you are because you've been so busy.
eligibility_rationale: Fails A2 gate: lacks barrier, agency, or emotional signals with a system interaction.

moment_text: My sister had it free month membership to Core Power Yoga, which just so happened to be right across the street from where I was staying. I could literally walk there. And I didn't really know what else to do with my time and with my energy and the sadness that I felt. And so I just started going to this yoga studio and I had done a little bit of gym yoga before, but didn't get it, thought it was kind of silly. I went to Core Power and their classes are heated, so it was hot and they're hard. So it was athletic and the music, I love music, so the music was pretty good. And the teachers were a little bit spiritual and talking about vibration and manifestation. So first class I went to, I go, oh, this is hot, this is hard. This is spiritual. This is musical. I'm, I'm listening. I'm listening, I'm gonna come back tomorrow. So I ended up coming back the next day and then the next day and then the next, and I started to kind of get it and I could, I could see why people come to yoga in times of maybe healing, like they come to yoga when they're in need of something or yoga finds them. It's interesting and a way I feel like yoga found me. It saved me from myself.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 gate: has emotional signals and a system interaction (peer_community).

moment_text: Yes, definitely the yoga. It had me listening. I was like, okay, I'm in. And I liked that it was a practice. 'cause what I missed from bodybuilding was the practice of bodybuilding. It really wasn't even about the stage. Yeah. That was like the, the culmination point, what we were working for. But it was a practice showing up every day to the gym, cooking the food, going to bed early, taking your supplements. I mean, this is a total practice and a way of living. And now I could see the parallel with, with yoga. If I kept practicing, my body kept opening up more. My mind kept opening up. It was quieting. It was like I could see this sort of pathway in front of me. And what I liked about it is that there is no best yogi in the world. So there was nobody for me to compete with anymore. It was just, it was a practice for myself.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 gate: has emotional signals and a system interaction (peer_community).

moment_text: Uh, so I kept going for a month, and at the end of the month I go up to the front desk and speak to the manager, and I have tears in my eyes and I say, I'm, this is my last class for my month. And I, I'm really sorry, but I can't afford a membership and I just come for drop in classes when I, when I can afford it. And she said, well, we do have this program where if you clean the studio once a week, then you can have a free membership. Right away I jumped at the opportunity because I was like, whatever will help me keep practicing, I will be here. Turned out to be my most favorite time. In my whole week I was working at F 45, working at another personal training studio, and then I would come at night to clean a studio. Sometimes at 12, 1:00 AM after everybody was done, I would be finished with my jobs and then head out the door and clean the studio.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 gate: has barrier signals (cost_constraint).

moment_text: You know, it was such a beautiful time for me to be in that space that gave me what felt like me back. It gave me my practice and my devotion and just sort of this way that I didn't quite understand, but I knew I liked it and I'd be in there listening to my teachers, you know? My, my headphones as I was cleaning rom das and, uh, you know, the spear Alan Watts and all these spiritual Deepak Chopra, you know, all those people. Mostly, mostly M das though. That's my, that's my main man. And I was scrubbing toilets at a yoga studio and cleaning showers and locker rooms. And I, it was just thinking of, again, humbling moments, right? And wishing that people who was looking at me. The second in the world, one of the best bodybuilders in the world. I wish they could see me scrubbing toilets right now, and I wish that they could see and feel how happy I actually am to be doing this. And it was this way. I, it was maybe, maybe it was like my first moment and I'm, I'm just kind of thinking about this. I had never said it in this way, but my first moment of service giving back and I, I haven't really thought about it like that before, but I think that's why it felt so good.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 gate: has emotional signals and a system interaction (peer_community).

moment_text: And after cleaning the studio for about a year, I continued practicing. And then I went into yoga teacher training and wrote in, and I got a little scholarship, a little bit of money off, which was so great, and ended up teaching at the same studio that I started cleaning for. Um, I remember the day, it was January 1st, 2020, and I, I continued to clean even after I started working there because I just, it.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 gate: has emotional signals and a system interaction (workplace).

moment_text: I just liked it. Yeah, it sounds like it kind of became a ritual for you, you know what I mean? Yeah. A healing ritual, you know, getting your hands dirty, literally and figuratively, if you know what I mean. We're, you know, 'cause sometimes we can be so up here, but when you get down and you're on the ground, if you will, there's, there's ways to sort of Yeah, I, I get that as a healing thing. Personally, I get it. So I think it's great to sort of connect with that type of work.

moment_text: So three months later, COVID happens and everything shuts down. At first it was like, you know, no touching, no this, no that, no breathing. And then all of a sudden it was okay, no more yoga studios we're shutting down. Everybody's in lockdown. And so I continued to teach online. Uh, at the time, I, I started a course leading an online course, my little business called Conscious Champion, and it was a way for me to help the bodybuilding industry out and to help them develop a strong, healthy mindset and deal with the things.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 gate: has barrier signals (access_barrier).

moment_text: That come with me. A, a competitor comparing yourself to others. All of the self-doubt, the imposter syndrome, the, the real mental things that go on the body dysmorphia. So I was helping people with all of these things and also physically like gut health and spiritual health and energy work, chakra work.

moment_text: So that was what Conscious Champion was about, was mind, body, spirit really as it pertains to bodybuilding, but. It could go for any athlete. And in that course, I kept teaching yoga once a week because I thought to myself, if I'm so new, I'm three months into teaching yoga. If I stop now and I let this COVID thing stop me, I will probably just not teach anymore. I'll probably just revert back and into being a student, which is fine. But there was something in me that said, don't. Get comfortable with this. Keep yourself uncomfortable, keep teaching, keep going through the motions.
eligibility_rationale: Passes evidence gate A2 with agency signals (making_decisions, enduring) and emotional signals (anxiety).

moment_text: And I don't, and I'm so glad I did that because months later we ended up moving up where I live now to Cape Cod and after lockdown was over, I think it was like maybe 20. 21, I, I don't remember the exact time, but I started teaching at a, at a local yoga studio and really grew into myself as a teacher. And I'm so glad I didn't stop because multiple times a week I would have 25, 30 people coming like this. You couldn't fit another person in the room. And it was just so, I was so grateful that I didn't give up when I could have given up very easily 'cause it again. Then the service continued and then. Me wanting to be clear, continued and like kept helping me show up in my best light because I was like, you know, teaching yoga, I'm holding space. This is a privilege, this is an honor. These people are coming to me and this is like a huge honor, really. And so I wanted to show up to be the best channel, the best energy, the best mirror that I could be for them.

moment_text: I was working with mushrooms for years. I'd been developing a relationship with them because that was kind of that inflection point I talked about earlier and I thought, oh, there's something, there's something deeper here. This isn't just tripping and having a fun night. There's actually like real medicine and real work and looking at myself. And so I continued to work with mushrooms for years and in 2021 for my birthday present to myself, I signed up for a coaching program called Fit for Service. And they take you through five months or so of different zoom calls with coaches and this and that. And then the end of it was a summit in Arizona, in Sedona, and it was camping and then a whole sort of three day retreat where we're doing other work, you know, just like more, uh, spiritual work. But during that I ended up going on my very first medicine high and. Got introduced to my teacher and that, you know, looking back now, that really, really changed the course of my whole life.

moment_text: And that was four years ago. But I look back and I go, oh my God. That was the day, that was the day it all happened where I learned about prayer and about the alters and about Peru. And I just, you know, met him and I was like, I. It just me. You're not getting rid of me. I had this feeling like, okay, I found my person and I'm very, uh, coach oriented, you know, from cheerleading. I was really co really close to my coach with weightlifting. We were super tight bodybuilding. Me and my coaches were like, tight, tight. So when I find somebody, that's my teacher that's gonna take me where I wanna go, this is like one of the most important relationships in my life because we have friends that come and go, partners that come. Come and go. Places that we live, places that we work, come and go. But like the one thing that remains the same is your path and the way that you're walking it and the learnings and the teachings that go along with that. So like, my coaches and teachers for me are one of the top relationships in my life ever.

moment_text: So met him, experience some ceremonies and he actually gifted me my very first flute. I had this beautiful, I. Image of a flute during an ayahuasca ceremony. And you know, I had been looking it up on my phone and thinking, okay, I wanna get a flute, but I don't know what key, I don't know what maker, I don't know anything yet. I was so just like, I want, I just wanna make the sound. So I had been thinking about it, thinking about it, had this ayahuasca ceremony and in the ceremony, my vision, so there was a flute that was there. I was. Trying to relax enough, right? And just listen. And I kept telling myself, just listen. Just listen. And then all of a sudden, the ears, my ears, my eyes, my nose, my mouth, everything kind of came together and all the holes in my face became a flute. They just melded into this one thing and it became a flute. And then the universe was playing me. I became this instrument.

moment_text: And I think that's such a profound thing to think about because really we all are instruments of the divine and of the universe. And the universe really is playing us. It's playing through me right now. You know, it's like it's such a beautiful vision. So, told my teacher about this and I was leaving and he gifted me my first flute and on the way home. Yeah, really it changed, you know, that was the moment. And I am not a musical person. I, my whole life I was in weightlifting, not band. I had never picked up an instrument and I was told my whole life that I cannot sing. I did gymnastics and my sister took singing lessons and it was kind of a joke a little bit, but I didn't really care 'cause I was kind of into athletics anyway. And I got this flute home and I started to play it and make my first couple squeaks and started to kind of sound like something. And then. From there, it was like this whole world of potential opened up to me and I was like, oh my God, me, I can be musical. I can, I can make pretty sounds with something. And from there, I took this online training course that my teacher has and started to acquire more instruments and more instruments. I started to sing and open up my voice and just really be brave and live on the edge of everything that I knew.

moment_text: Twofold. For me, the first one is, is to be of service. And you know, we mentioned yoga, sound and ceremony. These are the ways that I serve right now. So whatever medicine I'm serving, people can work through whatever it is that they need to work through. And that could even mean giving somebody a smile in a grocery store because for me, a smile goes so far and it helps people feel so good.
